does: This paper presents a practical safety and security framework designed for enterprise AI agent systems in real-world deployment. The authors argue that safety and security in agentic systems emerge dynamically from interactions between multiple models, orchestrators, tools, and data rather than being fixed properties of individual models. They develop a unified operational risk taxonomy that integrates traditional LLM safety and security concerns with novel agentic-specific risks including tool misuse, cascading action chains, and unintended control amplification. The core contribution is a dynamic framework that operationalizes contextual risk management using auxiliary AI models and agents under human oversight to identify and mitigate risks specific to deployed agent systems.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research addressing AI safety and security in large language models, extending these concerns to the multi-component systems of AI agents. It responds to the recognized gap between safety properties defined for isolated models and the emergent risks that arise when models interact with external tools and orchestration systems in production environments. The paper connects to prior work on LLM safety, security vulnerabilities in AI systems, and tool use in language models, while synthesizing these disparate threads into an integrated framework. It also draws on operational security practices and risk management methodologies from broader AI systems research.
enables: This framework enables organizations to systematically identify and manage risks in deployed agentic systems beyond what existing single-model safety approaches provide. It establishes a foundation for developing and implementing automated monitoring and remediation systems for agent-specific failure modes in enterprise settings. The taxonomy and operational approach create space for future work on designing inherently safer agentic architectures, developing better detection mechanisms for cascading failures and unintended control amplification, and establishing governance structures for multi-agent systems. The emphasis on human oversight integration suggests pathways toward developing scalable human-in-the-loop approaches for managing increasingly complex autonomous systems in high-stakes domains.
